channel.title: Probability Theory on Utsab's Digital Garden
channel.description: Recent content in Probability Theory on Utsab's Digital Garden

item.title: Convergences in Probability vs Almost Sure
Deterministic Random Variable
item.description: A random variable $X$ is said to be deterministic (or degenerate) if there exists some constant $c$ such that the probability of $X$ being equal to $c$ is exactly $1$. Mathematically: $$P(X = c) = 1$$ In this case, the probability of any other value occurring is zero. Essentially, it is not “random” but to be inclusive in the definition we have this case where it’s deterministic.

item.title: Independent Random Variables
item.description: Two RVs X and Y are independent if knowing one tells you nothing about the other. Mathematically: $$P(X=x,Y=y) = P(X=x) P(Y=y)$$ Equivalent condition: $$ E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] $$

item.title: Law of Iterated Expectations
item.description: Mathematical Definition For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$, the law states: $$E[X] = E[E[X|Y]]$$ Breaking down the notation: $E[X|Y]$ (The “Inner” Expectation): This is the average of $X$ given that you already know the value of $Y$. It is a function of $Y$, meaning it varies depending on what $Y$ is. $E[\dots]$ (The “Outer” Expectation): This takes the average of all those conditional results, weighted by the probability of each $Y$ occurring.

item.title: Probability and Statistics
item.description: Total Expectation Theorem Well Known Distributions Law of Iterated Expectations Independent Random Variables Doubts Deterministic Random Variable

item.title: Total Expectation Theorem
item.description: Law of Total Expectation (Partition of Events) $$ \text{Let } {A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n} \text{ be a partition of } \Omega \text{ with } P(A_i) > 0. $$ $$\large \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X \mid A_i] . P(A_i) $$ Law of Total Expectation (Conditioning on a Discrete Random Variable) $$\large \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{y} \mathbb{E}[X \mid Y = y]. P(Y = y) $$ Law of Iterated Expectation (General / Tower Property) $$ \large \mathbb{E}[X]

item.title: Unique Properties of Gaussian RVs
item.description: All linear combinations of independent Gaussian RVs are Gaussian A Gaussian random vector is a random vector all of whose linear projections are Gaussian. If $\underline{X}$ is Gaussian , then $X_{i}$ and $X_{j}$ are independent $equivalent$ Cov(Xi,Xj)=0 (uncorrelated) RVs are jointly Gaussian if they constitute a Gaussian random vector. It is possible for random variables to be individually Gaussian but not jointly Gaussian. Jointly Gaussian is a stronger property.

item.title: Well Known Distributions
item.description: 1. Bernoulli Distribution Use case: Single experiment with success/failure PMF $$ P(X = 1) = p, \quad P(X = 0) = 1 - p $$ Mean $\mathbb{E}[X] = p$ Variance $\mathrm{Var}(X) = p(1 - p)$ Special Properties Indicator random variable Building block of Binomial distribution 2. Binomial Distribution Use case: Number of successes in (n) independent Bernoulli trials PMF $$ P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k} $$ Mean $np$ Variance $np(1-p)$
